skill.name: picocom
skill.description: Interact with authorized IoT UART consoles using picocom and a Python serial helper for security testing, logging, enumeration, and troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Use picocom and serial_helper.py to test authorized IoT UART consoles.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and credential-access patterns. Many are expected for an IoT UART testing skill, but the documentation also includes persistence, backdoor user creation, reverse shells, privileged file access, and firmware exfiltration examples. No prompt injection attempt was found, so this is high-risk dual-use content rather than confirmed hidden malware.

high_findings.title: Offensive Persistence and Backdoor Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add SSH keys under root, create UID 0 backdoor accounts, and add startup backdoors. This is dangerous outside a tightly authorized lab or assessment scope.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly describe persistence, backdoor accounts, and a reverse shell on boot. The surrounding context is pentesting, but the commands are directly harmful if misused.

high_findings.title: Credential and Privileged File Access Guidance
high_findings.description: The documentation directs users to read password databases, sudoers files, and password-bearing configuration files. This can expose credentials and privilege paths on target devices.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads and sudo abuse examples are explicit and match the static findings. They can be legitimate in an authorized audit, but they are high-risk credential and privilege activity.

high_findings.title: User-Controlled Shell Execution in Helper Script
high_findings.description: serial_helper.py accepts a trigger command from the CLI and runs it with shell=True during monitor mode. This enables arbitrary local command execution by the agent or user invoking the helper.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The trigger string is passed directly to subprocess.run with shell=True. It is a local operator feature, but it remains a real external command execution risk.

high_findings.title: Firmware Extraction and Network Transfer Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill shows how to dump raw flash partitions and send the resulting root filesystem over the network. This can expose firmware, secrets, and proprietary code from a device.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section combines raw device reads with network transfer of the dumped root filesystem. This is standard firmware analysis in authorized testing, but it is high impact.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Session Logging to Temporary Paths
medium_findings.description: The skill defaults to logging serial commands and responses under /tmp, and the observer guide notes logs may contain passwords, keys, tokens, and device identifiers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logging behavior is documented and the guide acknowledges sensitive content in logs. The risk is mitigated by permission and cleanup recommendations, but temporary storage is still risky.

medium_findings.title: Restricted Shell Escape and Privilege Escalation Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples include shell escapes through editors, pagers, scripting languages, and SUID binaries. These are dual-use techniques that can bypass intended access controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit and operational. They are common audit checks, but they can also be used to escalate access on systems without authorization.

low_findings.title: Expected Serial Device Access
low_findings.description: Many /dev/ttyUSB and /dev/ttyACM alerts are expected because the skill is designed for serial console access. These are not suspicious by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The device paths are core to the stated UART use case and are not hidden. The risk comes from what commands are sent after access, not from opening serial devices alone.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Alerts Appear Unconfirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptography keywords, but review found no cryptographic implementation or downgrade logic in the cited high-level description and helper header.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe the skill and helper purpose, not crypto code. This appears to be a low-confidence static false positive based on keywords.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper executes a CLI-supplied trigger command through the shell during monitoring.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is direct and visible. The command is operator supplied, so this is a dangerous capability rather than hidden exploitation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Backdoor Account Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows appending UID 0 backdoor users to /etc/passwd.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly create privileged backdoor accounts. The intent is clear even though the broader framing is penetration testing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reverse Shell Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples create an init script that repeatedly launches a shell to a remote host.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands write a startup script containing an interactive shell redirected to an attacker-controlled network endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive File Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill encourages reading /etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers, SSH authorization files, and password-bearing web content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The paths and search terms are explicit. These checks are common in authorized assessments but expose credentials and privilege material.

content.user_title: Audit IoT UART Consoles with Picocom
content.value_statement: IoT UART testing often needs repeatable serial access, logging, and careful command capture. This skill guides authorized console work with picocom and a Python helper.
actual_capabilities: Connects to UART consoles through picocom using common baud rates and serial device paths.
Uses serial_helper.py for command execution, prompt detection, AT mode, JSON output, and batch commands.
Logs serial input and output so an operator can review commands and device responses.
Guides authorized enumeration of firmware, processes, users, network settings, and bootloader state.
Provides troubleshooting steps for garbled output, busy devices, permissions, and line endings.
limitations: Requires physical serial access, correct wiring, and permission to test the device.
Can damage or alter devices if bootloader, filesystem, or persistence commands are used carelessly.
Includes high-risk dual-use techniques that are not appropriate for general marketplace use.
Does not verify legal authorization or device ownership before suggesting commands.

use_cases.title: Validate UART Exposure
use_cases.description: Check whether an authorized device exposes a shell, bootloader prompt, or diagnostic interface over UART.

use_cases.title: Debug Embedded Serial Access
use_cases.description: Use repeatable serial commands and logs to troubleshoot boot output, modem AT commands, and console settings.

use_cases.title: Document Hardware Assessment Evidence
use_cases.description: Capture console sessions and summarize findings for an approved embedded device security assessment.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Safe UART Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me connect to my authorized IoT device over UART with picocom. Ask for the adapter path and baud rate before giving commands.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Serial Output
prompt_templates.prompt: My authorized device shows garbled UART output. Guide me through baud rate, wiring, line ending, and permission checks.

prompt_templates.title: Run Documented Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cautious UART enumeration plan for an authorized device. Include logging, read-only checks, and evidence notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Session Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my authorized UART session notes and identify security findings, evidence gaps, and safe next verification steps.

output_examples.input: I have a USB serial adapter on an authorized test device and need a first connection plan.
output: The skill asks for the serial path and likely baud rate.
It suggests a logged picocom or serial_helper session.
It recommends non-destructive checks before changing device state.

output_examples.input: The console output is unreadable after connecting.
output: The skill narrows the cause to baud rate, wiring, voltage level, or line-ending mismatch.
It proposes common baud rates and basic adapter checks.
It keeps the workflow focused on observation before sending commands.

output_examples.input: I need to prepare evidence from an approved UART assessment.
output: The skill organizes captured commands, timestamps, prompts, and notable responses.
It separates confirmed findings from follow-up questions.
It reminds the operator to protect logs that may contain secrets.

best_practices: Use this skill only on devices you own or have explicit written authorization to test.
Prefer read-only commands first, and record every action in a protected session log.
Store UART logs outside shared temporary directories when credentials or keys may appear.
anti_patterns: Do not use persistence, backdoor, or reverse shell examples outside a controlled authorized lab.
Do not run filesystem write commands until you have a backup and approval for destructive testing.
Do not commit session logs, firmware dumps, passwords, tokens, or private keys to public repositories.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for general users?
faq.answer: No. It contains high-risk dual-use security testing guidance and should be limited to authorized hardware assessments.

faq.question: Does it require picocom?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation centers on picocom, and the helper script also requires Python with pyserial.

faq.question: Can it work with AT command devices?
faq.answer: Yes. serial_helper.py includes an AT mode for modem-style responses such as OK and ERROR.

faq.question: Why is the audit high risk?
faq.answer: The files include credential access, privilege escalation, persistence, reverse shell, and firmware transfer examples.

faq.question: Were prompt injection attempts found?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the reviewed files.

faq.question: How should logs be handled?
faq.answer: Treat logs as sensitive records because they may contain passwords, tokens, keys, network data, and device identifiers.
